course_description: Financial statement analysis, traditional procedures & current developments. Use of accounting data for valuation of firms.

requirements.prerequisites: (ACCT7101 or BSAC7101 + BSFN7401 or FINM7401) or (COMM7301 + 7501)
requirements.incompatible: BSAC7106 or CO355 or 832 or COMM3505 or 7306

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
This course is an introduction to firm valuation, using financial statement analysis and other tools. The main purposes of the course are to:

Provide an understanding of factors which influence the quality of financial information contained in financial statements

Develop analytical skills which allow an assessment of the quality of financial information and an understanding of firm performance and financial position

Provide an overview of the valuation process

Understand how financial statements are used in the valuation process

Identify and enhance understanding of the various tools that are used at each stage of the valuation process

Develop skills in applying the tools when valuing a firm and its equity

Help you internalise a valuation model

You will leave this course with both a tangible product and an intangible product. The tangible product will be a report you have prepared that is the basis of your own buy-sell recommendation for a share of common stock for a traded firm. The report is expected to be of sufficient quality to use as an example of your work when you interview for a job. If your report is of high quality, it will help you demonstrate to a potential employer that you have a solid understanding of the issues that must be confronted when managing a business and creating firm and shareholder value.

The intangible product will be your knowledge of the valuation process and the practical skills you develop in undertaking your own valuation.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand financial statements How they relate to the firm's cycle of financing, investment, and operations. How they are organised (e.g., by primary and secondary activities; by the degree that an item forecasts the future) Issues concerning the quality of financial statement as affected by management's reporting decisions (does the past help forecast the future?)

Profitability analysis using financial statement ratios The accounting models and rates of return to the firm, creditors, owners The Dupont model Separating firm level issues from financing issues Earnings to the firm The tax shield provided by debt financing

Competitor analysis and Strategy Industry's competitive environment Identification of competitors and benchmarking them with ratios from financial statements The issue of restatement Quality of earnings Identification of the firm's strategy (e.g., cost leadership or differentiated product)

Prepare pro forma financial statements The mechanics of doing so, based on sales forecasts, growth rates, and ratios Conversion of pro forma financial statement balance sheets and income statement into the cash flow information by using the accounting equation Picking the forecast horizon: forecasting the firms competitive advantage (disadvantage) period Understand how accounting information relates to the cash flows of the firm and equity holders

Earning based valuation models Free cash flow (FCF) at both the firm and equity level Residual income (RI) at both the firm and equity level Common errors made when computing valuation Cross-checking with the FCF or RI valuation with market- multiples
Use ratio analysis to determine a firm's financial risk and financial stability.
Identify good corporate governance practices and have a broad understanding of the effectiveness of corporate governance mechanisms.

assessment_summary.title: Group Project Part A
assessment_summary.due_date: 12 Apr 10 - 12 Apr 10 16:00; Groups and companies must be registered by 10/03/2010

assessment_details.title: Group Project Part A
assessment_details.due_date: 12 Apr 10 - 12 Apr 10 16:00; Groups and companies must be registered by 10/03/2010
assessment_details.task_description:
Understanding the Business: Prepare a strategy analysis and financial analysis of your selected company.

Strategy Analysis

You are required to undertake a strategy analysis of your firm using a 'top down' approach. You should start with an analysis of the economy followed by an analysis of the industry and discussion of where your firm fits within that industry. Finally, you should discuss the internal strengths and weaknesses of your firm. In undertaking this analysis you should refer to relevant theory. Your analysis should be historical as well as forward looking.

Financial Analysis

You are required to provide an historical analysis of your chosen firm. Discuss the quality of the financial information provided by your firm (you should provide evidence of the quality of your firm's financial information). In this section you also need to provide accounting ratios that illustrate the 5 year historical performance and financial stability of your firm. Sufficient ratios should be calculated and presented so as to provide a detailed description of your firm's performance and financial standing. The appropriate use of graphs and tables is encouraged. A discussion of your assessment of the overall past performance and financial position of your firm based on these historical ratios is required. Further you will need to compare these ratios to industry averages and/or competitor ratios to understand how your firm fits within its industry and to identify its competitive advantage.

Page limit: 10 pages.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

Submission:

The project is to be submitted to the Resource Centre of the Colin Clark Building (Building 39)

assessment_details.title: Group Project Part B
assessment_details.task_description:
Forecasting and Valuation

This section of the Group Project requires you to utilise the information you collected in Part A in order to identify a forecasting horizon for your firm and then to forecast pro forma financial statements for your firm over this horizon. The information from the forecast pro forma financial statements will then be used to value the firm. This will require the use of the valuation models we have learned in class. All assumptions underlying your valuation of the firm need to be listed in your report. Sensitivity analysis is required to be undertaken and reported.

Page limit: 10 pages
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: Group Project Part C
assessment_details.task_description:
Analyst Report

You are required to prepare an analyst report for potential and existing investors in your chosen company. Your report should reflect and summarise all of the information you have gathered and the analysis you have undertaken in Parts A and B of this project.

Page limit: 5 pages

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Business
Assessment Guidelines

Policy on Consultation for Progressive Assessment
Whilst staff members are genuinely sympathetic to assessment grievances, individual reviews of progressive assessment is not feasible on a large scale. Accordingly, following the release of marks, consultation will be strictly limited as follows:

Students will be seen only in designated consultation hours, or by appointment.

No consultation will occur on the day of the release of marks.

The consultation period will be limited.

Priority at all times will be given to those students who did not achieve the pass mark for that piece of assessment.

Perusal of Final Examination Papers

Students are permitted to peruse their final examination papers but should note that this is not an opportunity to seek remarking of papers. Please follow the procedure below:

Students visit the UQ Business School Reception area, and complete a Viewing of Examination form.

The original form is kept by the School Office and a copy is given to the student.

Completed forms must be submitted to the School Office by the end of the first week of the subsequent semester at the latest.

Examination papers of those students who have submitted a completed form to the School Office will be available for viewing during the following relevant session.

The sessions are scheduled as follows: in the case of first semester examinations during the third teaching week in semester two; and in the case of second semester examinations and summer examinations during the third teaching week in the following semester one.

The only opportunity for students to view their final exam paper is during the above sessions. Exam papers cannot be viewed by approaching individual staff outside of these times, except for prospective graduands (see below).

Students completing their final courses and graduating may wish to see their final exam before graduation. Such cases are handled on a case-by-case basis with the individual staff member.

The location and the hours during which papers may be viewed will be published on the School's website and will be available from the School Reception area.

The student will be given their exam paper and the suggested answers (or the marking guide) which they may view on site.

No exam papers may be taken out of the viewing room.

No bags may be taken into the viewing room.

At the time of viewing the exam papers, the Course Coordinator or their authorised delegate must be available in the viewing room.

Appeal Against Results
Should a student feel that the mark allocated does not reflect the standard of her/his work, then she/he is encouraged to discuss the matter with the course co-ordinator. In the event of course-based disputes not being resolved with the course co-ordinator, the student may appeal to the relevant Head of School and then the Director of Studies of the BEL Faculty.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Other
School of Business
Guidelines

COMPUTING FACILITIES
Courses offered by the School of Business extensively utilise the internet and other computer facilities. Students should be familiar with these resources prior to undertaking a course. AskIT, available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT Desk, Level 2 Duhig Bldg at St Lucia, or Building 8 at Ipswich for further details.

It is the student's responsibility to attend classes and consult designated notice boards and websites for correct and up-to-date information regarding the course, including examination times. other_learning_activities_information:
The class environment:

Let us view our class as a learning community that will rely on student (and instructor) interaction to address a complex subject. Research shows that for complex subjects, people learn more when they interact because (a) feedback is provided (b) because different people have different knowledge bases and perspectives and (c) learning increases with active participation.

There are some other advantages from treating this as a learning community. The interaction is similar to that that is confronted in the work environment, so that it hones that important skill. And, for many persons, its more fun.

For our learning group to be successful, we need to treat each other with respect at all times and to share the opportunity to participate in discussion. Thanks in advance for you participation in this learning community.

The group project (getting started)

Teams: You will be asked to form a 2 to 3 person team to carry out your project. While some aspects of the project may seem better handled individually, experience indicates that students consider the interaction from being on a team as very helpful.

Choosing a firm: In this project you will value a firm. It would also help if your firm has an analyst following, since you can get some analysts' forecasts of earnings. Because this is an introductory course, it is best to avoid firms with unique problems. In addition, having a generic valuation lets you focus on applying the core principles in a familiar setting. Do not use firms from the mining, financial, and dot-com sectors. Other problem areas to avoid are very new firms and firms that have recently made very large (relative to their pre-acquisition size) acquisitions.

The firm needs to have at least three years of financial statement history

How to do well in this class

Reading: This class uses the text and its associated valuation program. The text provides practical advice on how to analyse firms' financial statements and conduct a valuation of a firms equity. It identifies sources of information, how to use that information to analyse the past, to forecast the future, and to compute the valuation. The text is also case based. We will be doing a number of these cases in class. You will be expected to read the set case/s and prepare answers prior to coming to class. Participation in class discussion on these cases will be an important part of the learning experience in our class.

Additional notes and examples on will be provided on particular topics. These additional notes and materials will be provided as handouts in class and/or via the course blackboard website.

There are also a number of text books on financial statement analysis that you may also find useful to read. A list of useful reference textbooks is provided in your class notes.

Other learning: This occurs from project related assignments, where you apply the tools being studied to your firm.

There will be a final examination in the exam period which will test your understanding and knowledge of all of the subject material covered during the semester.

During the semester, you should seek feedback concerning things you dont understand at the time the problem arises. Feedback can occur in class, visits to my office, or by email, or from your colleagues.

Finally, you should note that aspects of this course, and of valuation, are often frustrating because important decisions must be made without much information. This is compounded by the fact that you must forecast the firms firm performance and cost of capital. The ambiguity that surrounds valuation work is an important aspect of the analysts environment and cannot be avoided. But its the same ambiguity that managers of firms face, so this project should enhance you understanding of both analysts and mangers jobs.

Administration
All content related questions and specific questions re the administration of this course should, in the first instance be directed to the lecturer involved in delivery of the course. Content matters that cannot be dealt with by the lecturer should then be referred to the course co-ordinator. General administrative questions (for example centrally timetabled exams, room venues, etc) may be addressed to the UQ Business School Office on Level 2 Colin Clark Building, St Lucia Campus or Level 3 Building 12, Ipswich Campus.

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

late_submission:
Requests for the granting of extensions must be made in writing (not via email) to the Course Coordinator at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. The request must also contain documentation satisfactorily supporting the request. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application together with the Course Coordinator's and the student's signatures and a copy returned to the student.

Items (for which no extension has been granted) submitted after the due date and time, incur a late submission penalty. The penalty is at the rate of 5% (0.4 of a grade) of the total available marks for that particular piece of assessment, for each calendar day or part thereof that the item is overdue.

The above late submission information does not apply to honours theses, 8 unit research theses, 4 unit research projects and 2 unit research reports. For extension and late submission information in relation to these courses see section 5.4 of the course profile.
other_assessment_information:
ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

St Lucia students: Unless otherwise specified all assignments are to be submitted through the BEL Faculty Resource Centre (FRC) located on level 1 of the Colin Clark Building. Each assignment must be accompanied by the appropriate, fully completed FRC Assignment Cover Sheet, available from www.bel.uq.edu.au or the FRC. Assignments must not be faxed or emailed to the Course Coordinator without prior arrangement. All assignments will be stamped with the date of receipt, or if submitted after hours with the date of the next business day. Students must sign the cover sheet, acknowledging the plagiarism Statute of the University and must always keep a copy of their work. All group assignments must have ONE nominated representatives details at the top of the coversheet, with details of other group members listed on the lower section. Students should keep a list of their group members' full names and contact details. Note that students must produce their student ID card (or drivers license) to collect marked assignments.

Ipswich students: Use ASSIST: Assignment Submission and Tracking System
Step One: Print coversheet
Go to www.assist.uqi.uq.edu.au > Generate coversheet.
Step Two: Submit assignment
Scan the barcode on your student card OR enter your student number via the touch-screen keypad and press OK.
Scan the barcode on your assignment cover sheet.
Wait for submission confirmation on screen, and place your assignment into the numbered box as prompted on the screen.
The system will automatically reset for the next submission.
Note: that you must hand your assignment in at the campus at which you are enrolled.

SPECIAL EXAMS

Midsemester Exams
Midsemester exams are the responsibility of the Course Coordinator. The Faculty has no jurisdiction over midsemester examinations, hence all correspondence and documentation needs to be forwarded directly to the Course Coordinator. A student who fails to sit the midsemester exam at the scheduled time will be awarded a mark of zero, unless the Course Coordinator is supplied with documentation satisfactorily explaining the student's absence either prior to, or no later than two working days after, the midsemester examination. In such cases, the Course Coordinator has three options - (1) award a mark of zero if the evidence is judged to be insufficient or unsatisfactory; (2) hold an oral special examination; or (3) reweight other assessment items such as the final examination. There are no written special mid-semester examinations in the UQ Business School. The choice as to whether there is an oral special midsemester examination or a reweighting of assessment is at the discretion of the course coordinator and not the individual student involved.

Final Exams
A student who is unable to sit or complete a scheduled final examination must apply for a special examination no later than 5 days after the date the original examination was held. Applications should be lodged at a Student Centre for submission to the Examinations Section, who will forward it to the appropriate Faculty/School administration. For evidence required and time limits see Assessment Rules 1A.14 as listed in the University of Queensland Handbook. Faculties may approve applications for special examinations under the broad headings medical, compassionate or exceptional circumstances. Special examinations are not automatically granted. All applications are assessed on a case-by-case basis and may require an interview where it is considered necessary to appraise the student's position in more detail. Directors of Studies will identify and provide appropriate advice to students applying repeatedly for special examinations, including referral to Student Support Services and the Disability Program.
